title: Phrasal Verbs in Everyday Life
learningObjectives: Understand basic phrasal verbs in context
Learn how phrasal verbs are used in daily conversations
Recognize common phrasal verbs in simple stories
Build confidence using phrasal verbs naturally

sections.title: What Are Phrasal Verbs?
sections.content:
Phrasal verbs are very common in English. They are verbs with a small word after them, like <get up>, <look for>, or <turn on>. The small word changes the meaning of the verb.

For example, look means to see, but <look for> means to search. Let's see how people use phrasal verbs every day.

sections.title: A Morning Routine
sections.content:
Every morning, Tom wakes up at 7 o'clock. He gets up slowly and turns on the light. He puts on his clothes and goes downstairs. He <sits down> at the table and picks up his phone to check messages. Then he goes out to catch the bus.

Notice the phrasal verbs:
• <Wake up> - stop sleeping
• <Get up> - leave the bed
• <Turn on> - start a machine or light
• <Put on> - wear clothes
• <Sit down> - take a seat
• <Pick up> - take something with your hand
• <Go out> - leave the house

sections.title: At School
sections.content:
At school, the teacher says: Please <sit down> and <take out> your books. The students look at the board. When they don't understand, they ask the teacher: Can you <slow down>, please? At the end of class, the teacher says: Please <hand in> your homework and <clean up> your desk.

Phrasal verbs at school:
• Take out - remove from a bag
• <Look at> - direct your eyes to something
• <Slow down> - go less fast
• <Hand in> - give to the teacher
• <Clean up> - make tidy

sections.title: More Examples
sections.content:
Example 1: <I need to find out what time the movie starts.> (find out = discover)

Example 2: <Can you turn off the TV? It's too loud.> (turn off = stop a machine)

Example 3: <She always comes back home at 5 PM.> (come back = return)

sections.title: At Home
sections.content:
We use many phrasal verbs at home:

In the kitchen: <Put away> the dishes after washing them. <Take out> the rubbish every evening. Clean up the table after dinner.

In the living room: Turn on the TV to watch your favourite show. Turn off the lights when you leave. <Sit down> on the sofa to relax.

Phrasal verbs at home:
• <Put away> - Place something where it belongs
• <Take out> - Remove from inside
• <Clean up> - Make a place tidy
• <Turn on/off> - Start or stop a device

sections.title: With Friends
sections.content:
When you spend time with friends, you use phrasal verbs too:

Let's hang out this weekend! Tom <calls up> his friend. They meet up at the park. They <walk around> and talk. Later, they sit down at a café. Tom picks up the menu and looks at it.

Phrasal verbs with friends:
• <Hang out> - Spend time together casually
• <Call up> - Phone someone
• <Meet up> - Come together at a place
• <Walk around> - Walk without a specific direction
• <Look at> - Direct your eyes to something

sections.title: Phrasal Verbs in Short Stories
sections.content:
Example 1: MORNING - <I woke up late today. I got up quickly, put on my shoes, and ran out the door.>

Example 2: SCHOOL - <The teacher asked us to take out our books. We looked at page 10 and wrote down the answers.>

Example 3: EVENING - <After dinner, I cleaned up the kitchen, turned off the lights, and went to bed.>

sections.title: Key Takeaways
sections.content:
Remember:
• Phrasal verbs = verb + small word (up, <down>, on, off, out, in)
• They are very common in everyday English
• The small word changes the meaning of the verb
• Practice using them in simple sentences
• You already know many: <wake up>, <sit down>, <turn on>!

keyVocabulary.example: I wake up at 7 every morning.

keyVocabulary.definition: To leave the bed and stand
keyVocabulary.example: She gets up early on weekdays.

keyVocabulary.definition: To start a machine or light
To stop a machine or light
keyVocabulary.example: Turn off the TV before bed.
Put on your jacket, it's cold.

keyVocabulary.definition: To take something with your hand
keyVocabulary.example: Pick up your bag from the floor.
I need to find out the answer.

grammarPoints.explanation: A phrasal verb is a verb + particle (preposition or adverb). The particle changes the meaning.
commonMistakes: Forgetting the particle: 'I woke at 7' should be 'I woke up at 7'
